questions.text: I believe that over-preparation can signal a lack of trust in one's own ability to adapt.
I tend to miss the political subtext in professional communications.
I find myself noticing the cadence and rhythm of a conversation, not just the words.
I find that I adapt to the tone already present in groups I join.
I experience anticipation as an almost unbearable physical buzz.
I experience change as something to be navigated calmly rather than something that triggers alarm.
I hold the view that minimizing my own footprint allows more room for others.
I feel that the moral demand of suffering is the experience that gives my sense of justice its emotional foundation and its motivating power.
I hold the view that a detached, methodical engagement with human suffering is the posture that most effectively combines clarity of thought with depth of commitment.
I see myself as the captain of whatever ship I am on.
I often help the group sit with ambiguity until clarity emerges together.
I routinely spend my free time on activities that benefit me personally.
I notice that my emotional reactions rarely interfere with my ability to function.
I'd rather commit quickly to a direction and learn from the results than test endlessly.
I find that I am better at identifying what someone actually needs when I set aside the emotional coloring of their situation.
I tend to miss the moment when a conversation shifts from casual to serious.
I routinely budget for things that enhance my personal quality of life.
I plan my meals, workouts, or daily routines in advance.
